does: This paper presents circuit tracing, a method for revealing and analyzing the computational graphs that underlie how language models generate responses to prompts. The approach enables researchers to trace step-by-step computations through a model, mapping out which components (neurons, attention heads, layers) activate in sequence and how information flows from input to output. By decomposing a model's forward pass into interpretable computational pathways, the method provides concrete visibility into the mechanisms driving specific model behaviors and predictions, moving beyond black-box analysis toward understanding the actual circuits responsible for individual outputs.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability tradition that has sought to understand neural networks by identifying interpretable subcomponents and their interactions. It builds on earlier circuit analysis methods and activation tracing techniques that identified important neurons and attention patterns, as well as on work in feature attribution and saliency analysis. The paper responds to the broader challenge in interpretability research of moving from aggregate statistical understanding toward fine-grained causal understanding of how information transforms through deep networks, combining insights from both classical neuroscience-inspired unit analysis and recent advances in attention visualization.
enables: Circuit tracing creates foundations for numerous downstream applications in AI safety and model debugging. By making computational pathways explicit, it enables systematic detection of spurious correlations, systematic biases, and unintended reasoning shortcuts in language models. The method could support adversarial robustness research by identifying vulnerable circuits, facilitate model editing by pinpointing which computations to modify, and accelerate safety work by making it easier to audit whether models are reasoning as intended. It also opens possibilities for mechanistic understanding of emergent capabilities and alignment problems, and could inform more targeted training interventions based on understanding model internals.
